coredata.dc:title: The carbon dots modified ZnO films photodetector with broadband and fast photoresponse
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Jiale
Chen, Changheng
Jin, Minkun
Zhu, Yao
Zhang, Niumiao
coredata.dc:description: The ZnO films is a common candidate for photodetectors. However, the performance of photodetectors based on ZnO films fabricated by traditional sol-gel spin coating method is restricted to narrow absorption range and persistent photoconductance caused by defects. Herein, the carbon quantum dots (CDs) prepared by hydrothermal method are hybridized with the ZnO film to fabricate the ZnO/CDs hybrid photodetector. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) measurement confirmed that the existence of ZnO and CDs as well as the fabrication of ZnO/CDs hybrid structure. Owing to the wide absorption range of CDs, the ZnO/CDs photodetector demonstrated broadband photoresponse from UV (300–360 nm) to visible range (400–600 nm) compared with the ZnO photodetector. Meanwhile, the ZnO/CDs photodetector possesses the faster response that the rise time decreased from 180.93 to 5.94 s and the decay time reduced from 329.05 to 0.77 s. This work provides a useful approach to improve the optoelectronic performance of ZnO films photodetectors fabricated by sol-gel spin coating method.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnO
Carbon quantum dot
Sol-gel
Photodetection
Broadband

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of CDs, ZnO film and ZnO/CDs hybrid film.
(a) SEM image of ZnO film. (b) SEM image of ZnO/CDs hybrid film.

(a) TEM image of ZnO film. (b) TEM image of CDs. (c) HR-TEM image of the ZnO/CDs interface. (d) Scanning area for energy dispersive X-Ray spectroscopy (EDX) analysis and the Zn, O, C mapping images of ZnO/CDs hybrid film.
(a) Schematic model of the ZnO/CDs hybrid photoconductive photodetector. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, ZnO film, ZnO/CDs hybrid film and Tauc's plot (inset) of ZnO film.

(a) Logarithmic plot of I–V curves of the two different devices illuminated with a light of 365 nm and under dark conditions. (b) Time-dependent response of the hybrid device measured at a bias of 5 V. (c) Logarithmic plot of I-t curves of the ZnO and ZnO/CDs hybrid photodetectors measured at a bias of 5 V under 470 nm, 515 nm and 635 nm light illumination. (d) Logarithmic plot of responsivity and detectivity of the hybrid device measured at a bias of 5 V.
The normalized I-t curves of (a) ZnO device and (b) ZnO/CDs hybrid device at 5 V bias voltage under 365 nm light illumination.
The schematic illustration of the transfer process of (a) ZnO films photodetector and (b) ZnO/CDs hybrid films photodetector.
Comparison of the characteristic parameters of the ZnO/CDs hybrid films photodetector and ZnO-based photodetectors.

head.ce:title: The carbon dots modified ZnO films photodetector with broadband and fast photoresponse

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Photonics & Photon-Technology and School of Physics, Northwest University, Xi'an, 710127, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Photonics & Photon-Technology and School of Physics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Photonics & Photon-Technology and School of Physics, Northwest University, Xi'an, 710127, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The ZnO films is a common candidate for photodetectors. However, the performance of photodetectors based on ZnO films fabricated by traditional sol-gel spin coating method is restricted to narrow absorption range and persistent photoconductance caused by defects. Herein, the carbon quantum dots (CDs) prepared by hydrothermal method are hybridized with the ZnO film to fabricate the ZnO/CDs hybrid photodetector. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) measurement confirmed that the existence of ZnO and CDs as well as the fabrication of ZnO/CDs hybrid structure. Owing to the wide absorption range of CDs, the ZnO/CDs photodetector demonstrated broadband photoresponse from UV (300–360 nm) to visible range (400–600 nm) compared with the ZnO photodetector. Meanwhile, the ZnO/CDs photodetector possesses the faster response that the rise time decreased from 180.93 to 5.94 s and the decay time reduced from 329.05 to 0.77 s. This work provides a useful approach to improve the optoelectronic performance of ZnO films photodetectors fabricated by sol-gel spin coating method.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, photodetectors (PDs) have been widely used in optical communication, medical imaging, environmental monitoring, optical sensing and other fields [1–4]. Ultraviolet (UV) photodetectors have attracted much attention due to their important application value in the fields of high temperature event monitoring, security and network communication [5]. With the development of the semiconductor industry, various semiconductor materials have been used for UV photodetectors, including Si [6], CdS [7], ZnS [8], ZnO [9], etc. Among them, ZnO is considered to be a favorite material for UV photodetector, owing to its wider band gap (3.2–3.4 eV) and large excitation binding energy (60 meV) at room temperature [9,10]. Over the years, the growth methods of ZnO thin films include pulsed laser deposition, chemical vapor deposition, magnetron sputtering, ink-jet printing, sol-gel spin coating and so on [11]. The sol-gel spin coating method was chosen to fabricate ZnO films in this work, since it is a simple, low-cost, and highly controlled way, not limited by the shape of the substrate as well, which is convenient for large-area film formation [12]. However, the ZnO films fabricated by sol-gel spin-coating method may induce the lattice defects, which results in persistent photoconductivity and increase the response time [13]. Meanwhile, the application of ZnO films in optoelectronic devices under visible light is limited. Therefore, it is better to choose a surface functionalization of ZnO films with semiconductor materials to obtain a broadband and fast response ZnO-based photodetector.

Colloidal quantum dots (QDs), as a branch of semiconductor materials, with their high extinction coefficient and size tunable bandgap, are commonly used to broaden the spectrum and improve the sensitivity of two-dimensional materials photodetectors [14]. Actually, most of the commonly colloidal QDs are severely toxic for the use of heavy metal elements in their production such as Hg and Pb [15], impeding its application in optoelectronic device. In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CDs) as a substitute for colloidal QDs have attracted much attention for its low toxicity, green synthesis method, chemical stability, low cost, tunable photoluminescence [16]. Compared with traditional semiconductor materials, CDs exhibit zero-dimensional confinement, which promotes inter sub-band transitions between the quantized energy levels, which gives rise to broadband photodetection sensitivity [17]. According to previous researches, CDs can improve the surface defects of ZnO nanoparticles and enhance the carrier separation at the CDs/ZnO nanoparticles interface [18]. Furthermore, Kalytchuk et al. [19] observed that CDs can be used to sensitize a Si photodetector to the UV range. Therefore, it is a wise choice to use CDs improve the performance of ZnO optoelectronic devices.

In this work, hybrid devices consisting of ZnO film and CDs were fabricated. The CDs solution prepared by hydrothermal method was used to handle with the shortcomings of single detection ability and long response time caused by wide band gap and defects of ZnO film prepared by traditional sol-gel spin-coating method. The crystal structure was identified by the X-ray diffractions (XRD), and the micro-structure was characterized through scanning electron microscope (SEM) and TEM. The photodetector performances were measured by source meter system. The results indicated that the hybrid photodetector, possess wider working range and shorter response time compared with pristine ZnO film photodetector. The improved photodetection may be attributed to the reason that CDs weaken the adsorption of oxygen on the surface of ZnO film. This work provides a useful idea for improving the performance of ZnO film photodetectors.

All the reagents were analytical grade and used without further purification. CDs were synthesized through hydrothermal method. In the experiment, urea aqueous solutions consisted of 100 mL DI water and 2 mmol urea was prepared to synthesize CDs, and 1 mmol citric acid was added into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave with urea aqueous solutions for hydrothermal treatments at 180 °C for 6 h. After cooled to room temperature, the reactions yielded a brown solution with precipitation, which was subjected to centrifugation to separate the large carbon particles. Finally, the CDs solution was received by diluting the brown filtrate with absolute ethanol.

A ZnO layer was deposited onto a glass substrate (1.0 cm × 1.5 cm) by a common sol-gel method. The zinc acetate 2-hydrate [Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O] was dissolved in a 2-methoxyethanol (C3H8O2) and monoethanolamine (MEA) solution. The molar ratio of MEA and Zn2+ ions was 1:1, and the concentration of Zn2+ ions was added at 0.5 M. Then, put the mixture in a magnetic stirrer, and stirred at 60 °C for 2 h to obtain a transparent sol-gel solution. The solution was used for spin-coating ZnO film on a glass substrate at 3000 rpm for 20 s. Subsequently, the films were heated at 280 °C to evaporate unwanted residuals, the same heating and coating processes were repeated five times to achieve ZnO film. To obtain ZnO/CDs hybrid film, the CDs solution was spin-coated on ZnO film at 1000 rpm for 20 s, and drying at 60 °C for 5 min. To achieve the desired number of CDs, the same heating and coating processes were repeated three times. Finally, the films were annealed in air atmosphere at 350 °C for an hour. The photodetector devices based on the ZnO/CDs film were fabricated by using conductive silver paste to drop two Ag electrodes (5 mm apart) for photoelectric measurements.

The XRD analysis data were collected within 10° ≤ 2θ ≤ 80°, and a Rigaku-Dmax 3C powder diffract-meter with Cu-Kα (λ = 1.5406 Å) radiation was used. The Hitachi SU-8010 field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM), FEI TF-20 TEM (acceleration voltage = 200 kV) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) were used to measure the morphology and micro-structures of selected particles and films. Meanwhile, a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Cary 5000) was adopted to obtain the absorption spectra.

The steady-state electrical performances were measured by a KEYSIGHT B2901A source meter. In order to measure the response (rise and decay) time of the photodetector devices, a DHC GCI-73 multifunctional precision electronic timer and a DHC GCI-7103M − B shutter were adopted to switch and control the light.

The XRD patterns of CDs, ZnO film and ZnO/CDs hybrid film were shown in Fig. 1 . The diffraction peaks of ZnO film are consistent with the standard ZnO data (JCPDS# 36–1451), revealing that the synthesized ZnO is a hexagonal wurtzite structure [20]. The obvious diffraction peaks of ZnO appeared at 34.2° (002) and 72.2° (004) indicate that the c-axis preferred orientation growth of the ZnO film, which is perhaps due to the influence of the experimental procedure, the rotating speed of the spin coater, the choice of solvent and the precursor concentration[21–23]. Since the number of CDs was too small, it could not be clearly observed in the XRD pattern of hybrid film, so SEM and TEM were used to investigate the microstructures.

Fig. 2 (a) displayed SEM images of the ZnO film, indicating that the surface of the ZnO film is very dense and uniform. Different from the single ZnO film, the SEM image of ZnO/CDs hybrid film becomes blurry after loading the CDs shown in Fig. 2(b), which is due to the size of the CDs is too small to be clearly photographed with SEM. Raman spectroscopy is commonly used to confirm the presence of carbon in hybrid structures [24]. The characteristic peaks of Raman spectra centered at 420, 565, 790 and 1100 cm−1 (in Fig. S1a) are associated with the ZnO. Three characteristic peaks at 420, 1335 and 1590 cm−1 (in Fig. S1b) have been clearly determined in ZnO/CDs film, which are attributed to ZnO, D band and G band of CDs [17], respectively, confirming the formation of the composite.

Furthermore, the TEM characterization was also applied to confirm the existence of CDs and the combination of ZnO film and CDs. According to Fig. 3 (a) and (b), the ZnO films are made of stacked crystalline grains and the diameters is around 10–20 nm as depicted. The composition of ZnO films is similar to the composition of electrospun nanofibers which has been reported [25], and the size distribution of CDs falls between 1 nm and 5 nm in diameter. As shown in the HR-TEM image (Fig. 3(c)), the CDs is tightly attached to the ZnO (002) phase, suggesting the CDs successfully covers the surfaces of the ZnO particles. The TEM mapping images of the ZnO/CDs hybrid film are shown in Fig. 3(d), which is devoted to analysis the elemental composition: the darker color, the higher element concentration. The results indicate that the large particles are composed of ZnO, and the presence of Zn, O and C elements proved the sample was successfully synthesized.

A schematic model of the ZnO/CDs hybrid photoconductive photodetector is fabricated shown in Fig. 4 (a). In this work, ZnO films were prepared on glass substrate and CDs were spin-coated on ZnO films. Two Ag electrodes (5 mm apart) were dropped for photoelectric measurements, Ag paste as electrodes had been proven to be simple and high-quality in the previous study [26]. The UV–vis absorption spectra were performed to investigate the effect of CDs on the optical properties of ZnO nanostructured film. As shown in Fig. 4(b), the absorption band of ZnO mainly ranges from 300 to 360 nm and the absorption band of CDs comprises the strong absorption band centered at 310 nm and the weak absorption band located at 430 nm. In compared with the pristine ZnO film, the ZnO/CDs hybrid film not only has a strong absorption band from 300 to 360 nm, but also extends the absorption range to the visible region (400–600 nm) [27]. Such results fully illustrate that the CDs could extend the absorption range of ZnO nanostructured film. Besides, the optical band gap of synthesized ZnO was also fitted by equation (1) [28],
(1)
α h ν = A
(
h ν −
E g
)
n
where the α, hυ, A and E
g are the absorption coefficient, light energy, constant and the band gap energy, respectively. According to the inset of Fig. 4(b), the band gap energy of ZnO was calculated to be 3.25 eV through the Tauc curve, which is same as the previously reported [21].

Furthermore, to investigate the enhancement effect of CDs on the response of the ZnO/CDs hybrid device in the UV region, the current-voltage (I–V) and time-dependent current (I-t) curves of the two devices were measured. The semilogarithmic I–V curves in dark and illuminated conditions were shown in Fig. 5 (a). The 365 nm light with a power density of 0.57 mW/cm2 were used for illumination. For two different devices, the linearity of the curves indicates the formation of Ohmic contacts and the generation of photocurrent is all quite evident. It can be observed that after added CDs, the dark current exhibited the minimum value as negative voltage was applied, this phenomenon similar to Yan et al.‘s work [29]. Moreover, the photo and dark current decrements occurred on the ZnO/CDs hybrid device compared to ZnO based device because that an excess amount of CDs could lead a slight decrease in photoconductivity [30]. Although the photoconductivity decreases, the Ilight/Idark ratio of ZnO/CDs hybrid device remains at 104, which is still higher than most ZnO-based devices, as listed in Table 1 . The present ZnO/CDs hybrid device possesses a high signal-to-noise ratio, which is valuable for practical applications. Fig. 5(b) shows the time-dependent response of ZnO/CDs hybrid photodetector. The result indicated that the ZnO/CDs hybrid photodetector exhibits excellent stability and reproducible on-off switching properties upon 365 nm UV light at 5 V bias. However, in
Fig. 5(b)
, it may be observed that the peak current decreases over time and gradually stabilizes. It is reported that the crowding of carrier transport will force the photodetector show a poor photoresponse [31]. For ZnO/CDs hybrid photodetector, due to the existence of CDs, the contact area between the semiconductor (ZnO/CDs) and electrodes (Ag) becomes smaller. The point contact will result in the crowding of carrier transport, which will inhibit some photogenerated electrons and holes to pass through the interface in multiple fast cycles. This issue may be solved by expanding the contact area between the semiconductor and electrodes.

In order to further verify the effect of CDs on broadening the response spectral range of the hybrid device, the photocurrent responses of the two devices (ZnO and ZnO/CDs) at 470 (19.33 mW/cm2), 515 (7.45 mW/cm2) and 635 nm (28.38 mW/cm2) were measured and shown in Fig. 5(c). At 5 V bias, there is weak response under 470 nm light, moreover, there is no response under 515 or 635 nm light for the ZnO device. However, the response is detected, and exhibits excellent on-off switching stability for the ZnO/CDs hybrid device under the same condition. By comparing the response of the two devices in the visible region, it can be found that CDs can provide hybrid device with a wider working range.
(2)
R =
I light
−
I dark
P · S

Besides, the responsivity (R) and detectivity (D*) of the hybrid device under the wavelength from UV to NIR light were examined and displayed in Fig. 5(d). The R defined as photocurrent per unit of incident power. Basically, R was defined as follows.where I
light , I
dark , P and S denote photocurrent, dark current, the incident light intensity and the photosensitive area of device, respectively. Besides, D* is also important to evaluate the performance of photodetectors, was defined as follows,
(3)
D *
= R
S
2 q
I dark
where q is the absolute value of the electron charge.

For incident light at a wavelength of 365, 470, 515, 635 and 880 nm (23.08 mW/cm2) and voltage at 5 V, the R of the device are 3.70 × 10−2, 4.32 × 10−5, 1.80 × 10−5, 4.27 × 10−8 and 5.78 × 10−9 A/W, respectively. The D* for the incident light at a wavelength of 365, 470, 515, 635 and 880 nm are 1.08 × 1010, 4.91 × 107, 1.63 × 107, 3.32 × 104 and 5.69 × 103 Jones, respectively. The larger the values of R and D*, the better the photodetection performance of the device. What's more, the UV-to-NIR rejection ratio (response at 365/response at 880 nm) and the visible-to-NIR rejection ratio (response at 470/response at 880 nm) of ZnO/CDs hybrid photodetector is about 6.40 × 106 and 7.47 × 103, confirming that the ZnO/CDs hybrid photodetector extended the ZnO photodetector working range to the visible region.

In addition, the rise and decay time were examined, which are also the key performance indicators of a photodetector used to analyze the photoresponse speed. The rise time (τ1) and decay time (τ2) are defined as the time duration of the photocurrent signal from 10 to 90% and from 90 to 10% of its maximum, respectively. The characteristics of the normalized time-dependent photocurrents of the two different devices at 5 V bias voltage are represented in the plot shown in Fig. 6 (a) and (b). For the pristine ZnO device, the average rise and decay time was determined to be 180.93 and 329.05 s, respectively under 365 nm light, indicating a slow photoresponse speed, which had been previously reported in ZnO-based photodetectors. The surface state or body defects of the material may cause a longer response time [32]. For the ZnO/CDs hybrid device, the average rise and decay time were reduced to 5.94 and 0.77 s, respectively under 365 nm light, the photoresponse speed was remarkably increased. This result shows that CDs play an important role in improving the photoresponse speed of ZnO film photodetector.

As summarized in Table 1, the ZnO/CDs hybrid device performs better compared with that most of ZnO-based nanostructure photodetectors. Although some performance is not as good as other devices’, it exhibited higher photoconductive performance not only under the UV light, but also the visible light. It showed high photocurrent and dark current ratio of 5783 and 3454 under 365 and 470 nm light illumination, respectively, and faster response speed. Moreover, the R and D* values of hybrid device are also at normal levels compared with most devices. These results further demonstrate that this work is contributed to improving optoelectronic performance of ZnO-based nanostructure photodetectors.

It is reported that the surface of ZnO film has tendency to absorb oxygen molecules from air, and the surface defects can trap electrons, which leads to a decrease in overall conductivity [33]. The previous reported ZnO-based photodetectors have long response time for their slow desorption of oxygen process like the ZnO photodetector obtained in this work, the transfer process of electrons was shown in Fig. 7 (a). But for the ZnO/CDs hybrid photodetector, after introducing CDs, there are many CDs covering the surface of the ZnO film. On one hand, the response time is greatly reduced due to the decrease of oxygen adsorption [27]. On the other hand, due to the existence of CDs, the transmission of electrons between ZnO particles is hindered and the electrons are forced to bypass then transferring along the route with less resistance, as illustrated in Fig. 7(b). Thus the diffuse distance of electrons is extended, leading to the decrease in the decay time, which also explains the reduction of photo and dark current in the hybrid photodetector [42]. The above two results indicate that CDs can help for the improvement of response speed.

In summary, the novel ZnO/CDs hybrid device consist of ZnO film and CDs which were synthesized by traditional sol-gel spin coating and hydrothermal methods. The crystal structure was identified by the XRD. The TEM images manifest the size distribution of the ZnO crystalline grains and the CDs between 10-20 nm and 1–5 nm, respectively, revealing the fabrication of ZnO/CDs hybrid photodetector. After introducing CDs, the photoresponse band of ZnO film have been broadened from UV (300–360 nm) to visible (400–600 nm) range. The UV-to-NIR rejection ratio (response at 365/response at 880 nm) and the visible-to-NIR rejection ratio (response at 470/response at 880 nm) of ZnO/CDs hybrid photodetector is about 6.40 × 106 and 7.47 × 103. Moreover, the photodetection performance of the ZnO photodetector greatly improved: the response time under 365 nm is greatly reduced by two orders of magnitude, the rise time and decay time decreased about 30 times (from 180.93 to 5.94 s) and 427 times (from 329.05 to 0.77 s), respectively. This work provides a new perspective for improving the performance of ZnO film photodetectors and the ZnO/CDs hybrid photodetector shows a great potential for UV and visible photodetector.

Jiale Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, preparation. Changheng Chen: Formal analysis, Investigation, Visualization. Minkun Jin: Rietveld refinement analysis, Software, Formal analysis. Yao Zhu: Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, preparation. Niumiao Zhang: Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.11704312) and the Youth Innovation Team of Shaanxi Universities (21JP125).
